title: Design Payment System — Checkout, Transactions & Ledger
description: Design a payment/checkout system that processes millions of transactions daily with strong consistency, idempotency, and fraud detection. Covers the dual-ledger architecture, payment gateway integration, reconciliation, 3D Secure, PCI compliance, and how SAP's FI (Financial Accounting) module provides a familiar conceptual bridge.

description.value:
Functional requirements: (1) User initiates checkout — add payment method, apply coupon, confirm purchase. (2) Payment processing — charge the payment method (credit card, PayPal, digital wallet, BNPL). (3) Order management — create order record, update inventory, trigger fulfillment. (4) Refunds and partial refunds — reverse transactions and update ledger. (5) Receipt and invoice generation — email receipt, generate invoice PDF. (6) Payouts to merchants (for marketplace scenarios). (7) Dispute handling — chargebacks, fraud claims.

Non-functional requirements: (1) Consistency is paramount — double charges or lost payments are unacceptable. Use distributed transactions with compensating actions (SAGA pattern). (2) Availability: 99.99%+ — downtime directly loses revenue. (3) Latency: checkout flow < 2 seconds at P99. Payment authorization < 500ms. (4) Idempotency — retries must never result in double charges. (5) Auditability — every transaction is logged immutably for reconciliation and compliance. (6) PCI DSS compliance — card data is never stored in plaintext.

SAP Bridge: SAP FI (Financial Accounting) is the world's most mature financial system. The concept of a dual-ledger (transactional vs analytical), document splitting, reconciliation accounts, and clearing documents maps directly to payment system design. The key difference is: SAP processes financial postings in batch with explicit reconciliation steps; FAANG payment systems must reconcile in near real-time with streaming event processing. The accounting identity — every debit has a corresponding credit — is the same mathematical invariant in both systems.

The single most important architectural decision in payment systems is the dual-ledger pattern: separate the transaction ledger (what the user sees, what the payment gateway reports) from the accounting ledger (the double-entry bookkeeping that must balance).

Transaction ledger (operational store): Each transaction has a unique ID, amount, currency, payment method, status (pending, authorized, captured, refunded, failed), timestamps, and merchant/user references. Stored in a sharded SQL database (sharded by merchant_id or user_id). This is the 'source of truth' for day-to-day operations.

Accounting ledger (immutable append-only store): Every financial event generates accounting entries: debit the user's liability account, credit the merchant's settlement account. Entries are never updated — only appended with reversal entries. The sum of all debits must equal the sum of all credits at all times. This is stored in a write-ahead log or dedicated ledger database (e.g., a specialized append-only store like Stripe's internal ledger system or a PostgreSQL instance with SERIALIZABLE isolation).

Reconciliation pipeline: A periodic reconciliation job compares the transaction ledger against (a) the accounting ledger, (b) bank statement data from the payment gateway, and (c) the merchant's own records. Discrepancies generate alerts. This is identical to SAP's reconciliation process between FI sub-ledgers and the general ledger.

Payment orchestration layer: The checkout flow calls the payment orchestrator, not individual payment providers. The orchestrator: (1) Accepts the checkout request with an idempotency key (generated by the client on the first attempt, typically a UUID or request hash). (2) Validates the request (inventory check, fraud check, credit limit check). (3) Routes to the appropriate payment provider based on currency, amount, payment method, and cost. (4) Executes the charge via the provider's API. (5) Updates the transaction ledger. (6) Emits domain events (payment.succeeded, payment.failed).

Idempotency implementation: The idempotency key is stored in a database with a UNIQUE constraint. On the first request, the orchestrator INSERTs the idempotency key with status 'processing' and processes the payment. If the client retries (network timeout), the orchestrator SELECTs the idempotency key and returns the existing result instead of processing again. If the first attempt failed (e.g., insufficient funds), the retry with the same idempotency key returns the same failure — the client must generate a new key for the retry. The idempotency key must be stored with a TTL (24-48 hours) to prevent unbounded growth.

Exactly-once delivery challenge: Payment gateways provide webhooks for asynchronous status updates (e.g., 'payment.succeeded' after 3D Secure authentication). Webhooks can be delivered multiple times. Each webhook carries an event ID; the payment orchestrator deduplicates by event ID using the same idempotency pattern. This is the same concept as SAP's IDoc deduplication — each IDoc has a unique ID and is processed at-least-once with deduplication.

End-of-day reconciliation: Every 24 hours, the reconciliation system compares: (1) Transactions in the transaction ledger vs transactions reported by the payment gateway (Stripe/Adyen/etc). (2) Total amount settled to the bank account vs total captured transactions minus fees. (3) Refund requests vs refund confirmations. Discrepancies above a threshold ($X or 0.1% of volume) generate alerts for manual review.

Immutable audit log: Every state change is recorded in an append-only audit log: (1) When a transaction is created. (2) When a payment is authorized. (3) When a payment is captured (the actual funds transfer). (4) When a refund is initiated and completed. (5) When a chargeback is filed and resolved. The audit log is used for: regulatory compliance (PCI, SOX), customer disputes ('where's my money?'), and debugging production issues. This maps directly to SAP's change document (CHANGEDOC) and audit trail (AUDIT) concepts — every financial document has a complete history of who changed what and when.

PCI DSS scope reduction: The payment system never stores raw credit card numbers (PAN). Instead, it stores a token from the payment gateway (e.g., Stripe's tok_xxx or pm_xxx). The token is useless if the database is compromised. Card data is transmitted directly from the client to the gateway (Stripe.js, Adyen Web) — the server never sees the raw PAN. This is the 'tokenization' approach that Stripe pioneered and is now industry standard. PCI DSS scope is dramatically reduced by not storing, processing, or transmitting card data on your servers.

details: FAANG Interview Tip: The payment system design question is common at Meta (E6/E7), Amazon (L6 Bar Raiser), and Stripe. The interviewers are looking for: (1) Deep understanding of the dual-ledger pattern and why it matters. (2) Idempotency design — this is the most important technical detail. (3) Accounting invariants — knowing that debits = credits at all times distinguishes a payment system from a generic CRUD app. (4) PCI compliance awareness. SAP FI engineers have a massive advantage here — you understand double-entry accounting, reconciliation, and audit trails better than most FAANG engineers. Lead with that.
